GIS & Environmental Analyst
Alisson Capelli de Souza

Mapping the invisible to drive impact

I work with GIS and spatial data to monitor fossil fuel expansion, produce evidence for climate litigation, and build the maps and dashboards that help organizations act across Latin America.

View Work → Get in Touch

Geospatial & Environmental Profile

I have been working with GIS, environmental analysis, and spatial data for over five years, developing solutions for organizations that need to understand complex territorial dynamics and turn them into clear evidence for decision-making. My work focuses on spatial analysis, strategic mapping, and visualizations that connect technical rigor, political context, and the capacity to drive action.

I currently serve as Coordinator of Geotechnology and Environment at Instituto Internacional ARAYARA, the largest climate litigation NGO in Latin America. In this role, I produce the geospatial analyses and cartographic materials behind lawsuits, international reports, territorial investigations, and advocacy strategies related to fossil fuel expansion in Brazil and across the region.

Among the most relevant work in my career is the geospatial content I produced for The Money Trail Behind Fossil Fuel Expansion in Latin America & the Caribbean, co-published with Urgewald, Amazon Watch, and FARN ahead of COP30. The report gained international coverage and was cited by CNN and The Conversation. I also developed maps and analyses used in climate lawsuits against Brazil’s National Petroleum Agency (ANP).

My work spans a wide range of projects: from tracking offshore exploration blocks to mapping proposed thermoelectric plants in the Amazon and building interactive dashboards for international campaigns. Across all of them, the approach stays the same: rigorous spatial analysis, clear visual communication, and a critical reading of the territory to turn data into strategic information.

Events, Dialogue &
Professional Engagement

My work goes beyond technical production. I also take part in press conferences, panels, institutional meetings, and advocacy spaces where spatial evidence needs to be translated for non-technical audiences. I believe maps and analyses gain real power when they leave the specialist environment and contribute directly to decisions, public narratives, and advocacy processes.

At COP30 in Belém, I joined the ARAYARA delegation at the Amazon Climate Hub for the launch of the The Money Trail report. The maps and visual products I developed helped demonstrate how 297 banks channeled over US$138 billion into fossil fuel projects across Latin America and the Caribbean. Those findings were presented to journalists, partner organizations, and policymakers at press events in the weeks leading up to the conference.

I am also responsible for the geospatial material behind ARAYARA’s legal cases, including five civil action lawsuits related to 118 oil exploration blocks offered in ANP’s bid cycles. In this kind of work, the bar is not only technical — it requires maps and visualizations that can clearly communicate the territorial, environmental, and climate conflicts at stake, including for audiences like judges, prosecutors, and legal teams.

At the core of this work is the ability to bridge two worlds: the technical world where data is produced and analyzed, and the public and institutional world where decisions are made. My role is to ensure that translation preserves analytical rigor while being clear, persuasive, and useful in high-stakes strategic contexts.

Automation, AI & Python

An important part of my work happens behind the scenes: structuring scripts, automated pipelines, and processing workflows that make spatial analysis more efficient, repeatable, and scalable. Automating repetitive tasks allows me to reduce operational time, improve consistency, and direct more energy toward data interpretation and strategic deliverables.

In practice, this involves batch-processing satellite imagery, cleaning and standardizing spatial databases, integrating financial data with geographic layers, and organizing workflows for fossil fuel infrastructure monitoring and territorial impact analysis. For the Money Trail report, I built pipelines that cross-referenced corporate ownership records, exploration block geometries, and bank financing data — enabling both the cartographic production and the dashboard development for the project.

I also use AI tools on a daily basis — Claude, GitHub Copilot, and others — primarily to support code review, speed up technical documentation, and make exploratory analyses more agile. I see these tools as productivity and prototyping support, never as substitutes for critical analysis.

My core stack: Pandas, GeoPandas, ArcPy, Shapely, Rasterio, Xarray, GDAL/OGR, NumPy, Matplotlib, Folium, PostGIS, SQL, and Requests. I write code with a focus on reuse, clarity, and maintainability — building solutions that remain useful as projects evolve and new demands come in.

Dashboards, Web Mapping &
Spatial Visualization

A fundamental part of my work is turning complex analyses into clear, accessible, and genuinely usable interfaces. That is why I dedicate particular attention to developing interactive dashboards, web maps, and spatial visualization products that bring territorial data closer to audiences who do not work directly with GIS.

In Power BI, I build dashboards that combine geographic layers with statistical indicators, enabling readings by region, time period, theme, or analytical lens. In ArcGIS Online, I create web maps and StoryMaps that function as living products — updated as new information comes in and shared across teams and partners in different countries.

When standard platforms fall short, I also build custom solutions with Mapbox and Folium. These deliverables can take many forms: monitoring dashboards for oil and gas infrastructure, interactive territorial impact reports, or public-facing maps for strategic communications. For me, a good dashboard is not just visually polished — it needs to support reading, inform decisions, and expand the understanding of whoever is looking at the data.

Open for Opportunities

I am open to freelance GIS projects, consulting work, and collaborations in spatial analysis, environmental data, geospatial automation, and climate research.

Based in Brazil, working with teams across Latin America, Europe, and the United States.
